subject: History
overall_learning_objectives: Analyze the transformation of Delhi from a regional commercial center to a powerful imperial capital
Evaluate 'Tawarikh' as a historical source while identifying its social and gender biases
Examine the challenges faced by Raziyya Sultan in a patriarchal political structure
Describe the military and administrative expansion from Garrison towns to the 'Hinterland'
Compare the internal and external frontier policies of the Delhi Sultanate
Contrast the administrative strategies of Alauddin Khalji and Muhammad Tughluq regarding Mongols
Understand the 'Iqta' system and the role of Muqtis in medieval governance

lesson_plan.title: Delhi: From Tomaras to the Sultanate
topics_covered: Delhi under the Tomara and Chauhan Rajputs
The significance of 'Dehliwal' coins and commercial growth
Chronology of the five Sultanate dynasties
teaching_strategy: Map Analysis: Identifying the 'Seven Cities of Delhi'
Discussion: 'How did commerce lead to political importance?'
Visual presentation of the dynastic timeline
student_activities: Map marking: Locate Dehli-i-Kuhna, Siri, and Jahanpanah
Create a chronological table of the five dynasties and their key rulers
assessment: Short answer: 'Why was Delhi an important commercial center in the 12th century?'
Oral quiz on the sequence of dynasties

lesson_plan.title: The Authors of Tawarikh
topics_covered: Definition and characteristics of Tawarikh
Social background of authors and their proximity to the Sultan
The 'Circle of Justice' and the concept of 'Ideal Social Order'
teaching_strategy: Critical Thinking: Analyzing the biases (Gender/Birth right) in medieval writings
Diagrammatic explanation of the 'Circle of Justice'
Discussion: 'Why did Tawarikh authors ignore the lives of common people?'
student_activities: Interpret the 'Circle of Justice' and explain the link between military strength and peasant prosperity
Draft a short 'Tarikh' entry from the perspective of a court secretary
assessment: Defining 'Tawarikh' and its limitations as a source
Explaining the 'Ideal Social Order' based on birthright and gender

lesson_plan.title: Gender and capability: Raziyya Sultan
topics_covered: Raziyya's accession (1236) and Minhaj-i-Siraj’s views
Capabilities vs. Gender expectations in the 13th century
Comparisons with Queen Rudramadevi and Queen Didda
teaching_strategy: Debate: 'Capabilities vs. Social Conventions' in the context of Raziyya
Comparative analysis of how different female rulers handled their gender identity
Reading primary source excerpts on Raziyya’s removal from power
student_activities: Write a comparative note on Raziyya Sultan and Rudramadevi of the Kakatiya dynasty
Discussion: 'Why was capablity not enough for Raziyya to stay in power?'
assessment: Short response: 'What was Minhaj-i-Siraj’s opinion about Raziyya?'
Identifying the differences in how Didda and Raziyya were perceived

lesson_plan.title: Expansion: Internal and External Frontiers
topics_covered: Consolidation in the Hinterlands and Garrison Towns
Clearing of forests in the Ganga-Yamuna Doab
Military expeditions to Southern India under the Khaljis and Tughluqs
teaching_strategy: Visualizing a 'Garrison Town' vs. its 'Hinterland'
Mapping the military routes of Alauddin Khalji to the South
Discussion on the ecological impact of forest clearing for agriculture
student_activities: Differentiate between 'Internal' and 'External' frontiers in a table
Trace the southern military campaigns on a blank map of India
assessment: Explaining the purpose of the Doab campaigns

lesson_plan.title: The Masjid: Religion and Political Legitimacy
topics_covered: Architecture: Quwwat al-Islam and the Qutb Minar
The Mosque as a community center (Namaz, Khutba, Qibla)
Why Sultans claimed to be 'Protectors of Islam'
teaching_strategy: Visual analysis: Identifying structural elements (Iwan, Dome, Minaret)
Explaining the political utility of a congregational mosque
Discussion: 'How did building mosques help create a sense of community?'
student_activities: Label a diagram of a mosque with terms like Sahn, Liwan, and Qibla
Research the meaning of 'Khutba' during the Friday prayer
assessment: Explaining why the direction of Mecca (Qibla) was significant

lesson_plan.title: Administration: Khaljis and Tughluqs
topics_covered: The 'Iqta' system: Muqtis and their responsibilities
Bandagan: The role of elite military slaves
Comparing responses to Mongol invasions (Alauddin vs. Muhammad Tughluq)
teaching_strategy: Comparative Chart: Administrative measures of Alauddin vs. Muhammad Tughluq
Discussion: 'Why were slaves often more trusted than noblemen?'
Analyzing the failure of 'Token Currency'
student_activities: Fill a comparative table on 'Mongol Defense' strategies
Role-play: A Muqti explaining their duties to the Sultan's accountant

lesson_plan.title: The Sultanate in the 15th-16th Centuries
topics_covered: The Sayyid and Lodi dynasties
The emergence of regional states (Jaunpur, Malwa, Gujarat)
Sher Shah Sur: Administration and influence on the Mughals
teaching_strategy: Discussion on the decentralization of power after the Tughluqs
Evaluating Sher Shah Sur's administrative reforms
Review of the chapter through a comprehensive mind map
student_activities: Construct a mind map linking the five dynasties, administration, and expansion
Write two lines on why Sher Shah Sur is significant despite his short reign
assessment: Evaluation of the 'Khalji vs. Tughluq' comparative chart

teaching_aids: NCERT History Textbook: Our Pasts - II
Political Maps of the Sultanate era
Images of Sultanate architecture (Mosques and Minars)
Diagram of the Iqta system hierarchy
Comparison Table: Alauddin Khalji vs. Muhammad Tughluq
teacher_tip: When comparing Alauddin and Muhammad Tughluq, emphasize that while Alauddin’s measures were successful, Muhammad Tughluq’s measures were often visionary but poorly executed—this helps students understand the difference between planning and implementation.
